critique.summary: The article reports that Honda is discontinuing its electric vehicle (EV) programs, including halting development of new EV models and stopping production of the Prologue, a GM-built EV. The author argues that Honda's retreat from EVs risks leaving the company behind in the automotive industry's shift to electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles.
mainPoints: Honda has stopped development of its electric Acura RDX, Honda 0 sedan and SUV, and will cease production of the Prologue EV.
The company blames U.S. tariffs and Chinese competition but lacked a strong EV strategy from the start.
Honda risks falling behind in two major automotive shifts: electric drivetrains and software-defined vehicles (SDVs).
Legacy automakers like Honda mistakenly treat EVs as just cars with different drivetrains, ignoring the need for ground-up EV design.
Honda will miss out on learning opportunities in EV development, manufacturing, supplier networks, and customer feedback.
Software-defined vehicles, which allow over-the-air updates and advanced features, are becoming standard in EVs, but Honda has made little progress here.
Honda faces an identity crisis as a traditional internal combustion engine company in a market moving toward EVs and autonomous features.
Honda's competitiveness is declining, especially in China, contributing to significant financial losses.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims about Honda discontinuing its EV programs and halting development of specific models are consistent with recent reports from credible automotive news sources such as Automotive News. The challenges faced by legacy automakers in transitioning to EVs, including issues with platform design and software capabilities, are well-documented in industry analyses. The mention of Honda's financial losses and struggles in China aligns with publicly available earnings reports and market data. However, the article is largely opinionated and speculative about Honda's future prospects and strategic decisions without direct statements from Honda's leadership beyond cited earnings comments. Overall, the factual claims about Honda's EV program status are accurate, but some conclusions about the company's future are interpretive.
considerations: Honda's long-term strategic plans may evolve, including potential partnerships or new EV initiatives not covered in the article.
The article does not discuss Honda's investments or progress in hybrid vehicles or other alternative powertrains.
Broader market and regulatory changes could impact Honda's EV strategy and competitiveness in the future.
The article focuses on U.S. and Chinese markets but does not address Honda's position in other global regions.
The impact of supply chain issues or semiconductor shortages on Honda's EV development is not mentioned.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on Honda's decision to halt its EV efforts, with commenters debating the viability of EVs, the impact on legacy automakers, and Honda's strategic positioning. Opinions vary on the future of EVs, software-defined vehicles, and the automotive industry's transition challenges.

comments.author: small_model
comments.text: All the legacy automakers that haven't fully moved to EV's PROFITABLY will go defacto bankrupt within a few years, there will be some mergers to stay alive but it's game over. Tesla and China companies will own auto, with Tesla capturing most the profit, similar to Apple vs Android phones. Autonomy will further accelerate this.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately reflects the high stakes for legacy automakers in the EV transition and the competitive threat from Tesla and Chinese companies.

comments.author: Denatonium
comments.text: Calling the Prologue "Honda's EV" feels like a huge stretch. The Prologue was a rebadged GM vehicle that served strictly as a compliance car for meeting CAFE standards. Now that the CAFE standards have been rendered toothless, there's no longer a need for that deal.
comments.analysis: This provides factual context about Honda's limited EV efforts, highlighting the Prologue as a compliance vehicle rather than a core product.

comments.author: bryanlarsen
comments.text:
OTOH, it really looks like Toyota is Goldilocks. Most companies invested too much too early and had to write off a substantial amount, but Toyota is rolling into 2027 with a small but nice selection of EV's.

Over 25% of vehicles sold world-wide were electric in 2025, and that percentage is steadily increasing. So VW & Ford were "too hot", Honda is looking like "too cold" and Toyota might be the "just right" of the three bears.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a balanced view of Toyota's cautious but steady EV strategy compared to other automakers, which is supported by market data.

comments.author: alliao
comments.text: To be honest, I have every faith in Honda. It took them a long time to arrive at hybrid, but they were never about first to market, but they were always adamant about controlling the entire technology stack.. made their own transmission and everything. And engineering doesn't faze them, Honda just nonchalantly displayed a reusable rocket like it was too easy... EV is a little bit like AI nowadays, not much moat and possibly not challenging enough for Honda R&D so why not. I'll always be on the look out for Honda's next take on EV.
comments.analysis: This comment insightfully highlights Honda's engineering culture and approach to technology adoption, suggesting they may still innovate in EVs.

comments.author: throw7
comments.text:
I'm not anti-EV...

I don't have charging capability at my apartment or work. On occasion, I do 300 mile trips (adirondacks/nyc). Skeptical of winter performance. I have no interest in "frequent updates, slick infotainment software, and advanced driver-assistance systems". Frankly, no spare tire is a no starter for me also.
comments.analysis: Reflects common practical concerns about EV adoption barriers, such as charging infrastructure and software complexity.

comments.author: gcanyon
comments.text:
EVs are going to be an extinction-level event for carmakers.

As the buggy-makers failed to transition to making cars, and thus ceased to be, so too will automakers fail to transition to EVs, and thus end their viability as vehicle manufacturers.
comments.analysis: Uses historical analogy to emphasize the disruptive impact of EVs on traditional automakers, a perspective shared by many industry analysts.

comments.author: nytesky
comments.text:
Honda is an engine company at its heart. It makes very reliable, long lived engines.

They refine technology not really invent it (maybe invented VTEC). The transition to EV will be very gradual, I don’t even think we have enough rare earth metals and electrical grid capacity to go even twice as fast in adoption?

Honda is waiting for the standards and technology to settle out and become commodity technology, then they implement and iterate to a refined and reliable product.

It doesn’t seem like a winner take all market for EV? What would be the most? Perhaps I am ignorant on that part of market dynamics.

*edit for typos
comments.analysis: Provides a nuanced view of Honda's strengths and the challenges of EV adoption, including resource constraints and market dynamics.

comments.author: billfor
comments.text:
"Here, Honda is setting itself up for failure on the second disruption sweeping the automotive industry: the software-defined vehicle (SDV), which has core capabilities that can be upgraded and improved over time."

I'll pay triple for a non software defined vehicle that doesn't track me and can't be touched by the dealer once I purchase it. My one SDV (Tesla) is still on FSD from 2023 because the newer versions are terrible judging from the comments on the Tesla forums.
comments.analysis: Expresses a consumer perspective skeptical of software-defined vehicles and privacy concerns, reflecting a segment of the market.

comments.author: GianFabien
comments.text:
Smart doorbells and thermostats that upgraded in the night often became a nuisance or an expensive brick. But a faulty software upgrade on a car can kill you and others.

Car company execs need to take a chill pill followed by a reality serum. Monetizing subscription based basic features and delivering in-car advertising is the absolutely worst way to go.

As consumers we need to stop buying into the bells, whistles and trinkets and demand essential and safe transportation.
comments.analysis: Raises valid safety concerns about software updates in vehicles and criticizes monetization strategies, urging consumer caution.

comments.author: dbg31415
comments.text:
> It makes really good engines, and that's starting to matter less and less.

Maybe. But here's the thing... most cars today feel completely lifeless.

Honda knows how to build an engine and wrap it in a car that actually makes you feel something. That still matters.

Anyone here driven an S2000?

It's still the best car I've ever owned. Light, raw, grippy, and genuinely fun -- every drive felt like an event, not just transportation. (And it was still an affordable car!)

They killed it around 2010. I've never found anything that captures that same feeling since, at any price point.

So yeah -- Honda will always have a place in my heart. When they want to, they build something truly special.

Here's one of their marketing films they can use to find inspiration again.

* Failure: The Secret to Success - A Honda Documentary - YouTube // https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xOVig5H7UbM
comments.analysis: Highlights Honda's legacy of engaging driving experiences, lamenting the loss of this as the industry shifts to EVs.
